sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage: An optics bench uses a converging lens with focal length +10 cm. An object is placed 30 cm in front of the lens, and a screen is moved until a sharp image forms. Students use the thin-lens equation 1/f = 1/do + 1/di and magnification m = −di/do. They observe that the image is real, inverted, and smaller than the object.
questions.stem: What is the image distance for the object placed 30 cm from the +10 cm lens?

explanations.A: Correct. Using 1/f = 1/do + 1/di gives 1/10 = 1/30 + 1/di, so 1/di = 1/10 − 1/30 = 1/15. Thus di = +15 cm, a real image on the opposite side of the lens.
explanations.B: 7.5 cm comes from adding rather than subtracting reciprocals incorrectly.
explanations.C: 30 cm would occur for a different object location, not this one.
explanations.D: A negative image distance would indicate a virtual image, inconsistent with a screen image.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Distances for an object 30 cm from a +10 cm converging lens, producing an image 15 cm from the lens.

questions.stem: What is the magnification of the image?

explanations.C: Correct. Magnification is m = −di/do = −15/30 = −0.50. The negative sign indicates inversion, and the magnitude less than 1 indicates a smaller image.
explanations.A: A positive sign would indicate upright orientation, not the observed inverted image.
explanations.B: −2.0 reverses the ratio of object and image distances.
explanations.D: +2.0 is both the wrong magnitude and the wrong orientation.

questions.stem: Why can the image be projected onto a screen?

choices.B: It is a real image formed by converging rays
choices.A: It is a virtual image behind the object
choices.C: It forms at the focal point only

explanations.B: Correct. A real image forms where light rays physically converge after passing through the lens, so it can be captured on a screen. The positive image distance is consistent with that observation.
explanations.A: Virtual images cannot be projected directly onto a screen because rays do not actually converge there.
explanations.C: The image forms at 15 cm, not at the 10 cm focal point.
explanations.D: The object distance is positive for a real object placed in front of the lens.

questions.stem: If the object were moved closer to the focal point from outside f, what would happen to image distance?

choices.B: It would remain fixed at 15 cm
choices.C: It would become equal to the focal length immediately

explanations.D: Correct. For a converging lens with do greater than f, moving the object closer to f makes 1/do closer to 1/f, so 1/di decreases and di increases. The real image moves farther away.
explanations.A: The image distance does not decrease to zero; it grows as the object approaches f from outside.
explanations.B: Image distance depends on object distance and is not fixed.
explanations.C: The image distance is not automatically equal to the focal length.

questions.stem: Which description matches the image formed in this setup?

explanations.B: Correct. The positive image distance means the image is real, the negative magnification means it is inverted, and |m| = 0.50 means it is reduced in size.
explanations.A: A virtual upright enlarged image would occur for an object inside the focal length of a converging lens.
explanations.C: A real image from a single converging lens in this setup is inverted, not upright.
explanations.D: The image is not virtual because it forms on a screen.

questions.passage: A laser with wavelength 600 nm passes through a single slit of width 0.20 mm and projects onto a screen 2.0 m away. For small angles, the first dark fringe occurs at y = λL/a. Students measure the distance from the central maximum to the first minimum and compare it with the predicted 6.0 mm value. They then test wider slits and observe a narrower diffraction pattern.
questions.stem: What is the expected distance from the center to the first dark fringe?

explanations.A: Correct. Using y = λL/a with λ = 600 nm = 6.0 × 10⁻⁷ m, L = 2.0 m, and a = 2.0 × 10⁻⁴ m gives y = 0.006 m = 6.0 mm.
explanations.B: 0.60 mm is too small by a factor of 10.
explanations.C: 12 mm doubles the correct first-minimum distance.
explanations.D: 3.0 mm would result from using half the screen distance or doubling slit width.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Qualitative single-slit diffraction pattern showing a broad central maximum and weaker side maxima.

questions.stem: What happens to the diffraction pattern when slit width increases?

choices.D: The first minimum moves farther from the center

explanations.C: Correct. Because y = λL/a, increasing slit width a decreases the distance to the first minimum. A wider slit therefore produces a narrower diffraction pattern.
explanations.A: A wider central maximum would require the first minima to move farther apart, opposite the equation.
explanations.B: Changing slit width does not change the laser wavelength.
explanations.D: The first minimum moves closer to the center as a increases.

questions.stem: Why does diffraction become more pronounced for narrower slits?

choices.D: The slit width becomes more comparable to the wavelength
choices.A: Photons lose all energy at the slit
choices.B: The speed of light decreases to zero

explanations.D: Correct. Wave spreading is most noticeable when an aperture is not much larger than the wavelength. Reducing slit width increases the angular spread of the diffraction pattern.
explanations.A: Diffraction does not require photons to lose all energy.
explanations.B: Light does not stop at the slit; it propagates and interferes.
explanations.C: The screen distance is controlled separately and does not automatically change.

questions.stem: Which variable would increase the first-minimum distance if slit width and wavelength stayed fixed?

explanations.A: Correct. The first-minimum position is y = λL/a. With λ and a fixed, y is directly proportional to screen distance, so increasing L moves the first dark fringe farther from the center.
explanations.C: Increasing slit width would reduce y because a is in the denominator.
explanations.D: A shorter wavelength would reduce y.

questions.stem: Why is the small-angle approximation reasonable in this setup?

choices.C: The fringe distance is much smaller than the 2.0 m screen distance
choices.A: The wavelength is larger than the slit width
choices.B: The central maximum has zero intensity
choices.D: The screen is at the focal point of a lens

explanations.C: Correct. The first minimum is only 6 mm from the center while the screen is 2.0 m away, so the angle is small and sinθ ≈ tanθ ≈ y/L is a good approximation.
explanations.A: The wavelength is actually much smaller than the slit width.
explanations.B: The central maximum is the brightest part of the pattern, not zero intensity.
explanations.D: No lens focal-plane setup is described; the approximation comes from small geometry.
